questions.question: Define race condition and explain why it is a problem in concurrent systems.
keyPoints: Race condition is when program outcome depends on timing of concurrent operations
Results become unpredictable and often incorrect
Multiple processes accessing shared data without synchronization leads to data inconsistency
The order of execution affects final result making debugging difficult

questions.question: List and explain the three requirements for a valid solution to the critical section problem.
keyPoints: Mutual Exclusion: Only one process can be in critical section at a time
Progress: If no process is in CS, and some processes want to enter, one will enter without delay
Bounded Waiting: After a process requests entry, there is a limit on how many other processes can enter CS before this process

questions.question: Explain the difference between a binary semaphore and a mutex. When would you prefer one over the other?
keyPoints: Binary semaphore: can be signaled by any process, no ownership concept
Mutex: only process that locks can unlock, has ownership
Mutex provides better safety in recursive scenarios
Prefer mutex when ownership tracking is important
Prefer binary semaphore for simple signaling between processes

questions.question: What is busy waiting (spinlock)? What are its advantages and disadvantages compared to blocking locks?
keyPoints: Busy waiting: process repeatedly checks lock variable in a loop until it becomes available
Advantage: no context switch overhead, good for short wait times
Disadvantage: wastes CPU cycles, can cause priority inversion
Better for multi-core systems where spinlock holder runs on different CPU
Prefer blocking locks (sleep) when wait time is long or unpredictable

questions.question: Explain the readers-writers problem. What is the challenge in solving this problem optimally?
keyPoints: Multiple readers can access shared data simultaneously but writers need exclusive access
Challenge: maximize concurrency while ensuring data consistency
If readers keep arriving, writers may starve (starvation)
If writers get priority, readers may starve
Need balanced solution that prevents both types of starvation

questions.question: What is priority inversion? How can it be mitigated?
keyPoints: Priority inversion: higher priority process waits for lower priority process holding needed resource
Common in systems with priority-based scheduling
Solution 1: Priority inheritance - temporarily raise priority of holding process
Solution 2: Use ceiling priority protocols
Can lead to deadlock if not handled properly

questions.question: Differentiate between deadlock and starvation with examples.
keyPoints: Deadlock: processes permanently blocked, each waiting for resources held by others
Example: Two processes each holding one resource and waiting for the other's resource
Starvation: process waits indefinitely but may eventually proceed
Example: Low-priority process never getting CPU time in priority scheduling
Key difference: deadlock is permanent blocking; starvation may end eventually

questions.question: Explain the producer-consumer problem (bounded buffer problem). How do semaphores solve this synchronization problem?
keyPoints: Producer produces items and places in shared buffer; consumer removes items
Buffer has limited capacity - producer must wait if full, consumer must wait if empty
Use two counting semaphores: 'empty' (count of empty slots) and 'full' (count of filled slots)
Use one binary semaphore/mutex for mutual exclusion on buffer access
Producer: wait(empty), add item, signal(full)
Consumer: wait(full), remove item, signal(empty)
This ensures no overflow or underflow and maintains data consistency

questions.question: Explain Peterson's algorithm for solving the critical section problem. What are its limitations?
keyPoints: Software solution for two processes using shared variables 'turn' and 'flag[2]'
flag[i] = true indicates process i wants to enter CS
turn variable indicates whose turn it is
Entry: set flag true, set turn to other process, wait until other process is not ready or it's my turn
Exit: set flag false
Satisfies mutual exclusion, progress, and bounded waiting
Limitation 1: Only works for 2 processes
Limitation 2: Modern processors with separate caches may not work correctly due to memory coherence issues
Limitation 3: Assumes atomic read/write operations

questions.question: What are monitors in synchronization? How do they provide mutual exclusion? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: Monitor is a high-level synchronization construct combining data and procedures
Only one process can execute inside a monitor at any time
Provides automatic mutual exclusion - programmer doesn't need to explicitly write lock/unlock
Contains shared variables (monitor state) and procedures (operations)
Condition variables allow processes to wait inside monitor
Example: Bank account monitor with 'balance' and 'withdraw()', 'deposit()' methods
Withdrawal checks balance, waits if insufficient, then deducts
Automatic enforcement prevents race conditions on balance

questions.question: Discuss the dining philosophers problem and at least two solutions to avoid deadlock.
keyPoints: Five philosophers sitting at circular table, each needs two forks to eat
Each fork shared between adjacent philosophers - classic deadlock scenario
All pick left fork simultaneously - deadlock (circular wait)
Solution 1: Even-odd strategy - philosopher picks forks based on number (odd picks left then right, even picks right then left)
Solution 2: Maximum N-1 philosophers allowed to sit at table
Solution 3: Asymmetric pickup - odd numbered pick left then right, even pick right then left
Solution 4: Use a butler (extra synchronization) to control table access
All solutions break one of the four necessary conditions for deadlock
